mission.title: Install and Configure Kubeflow on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kubeflow is a powerful machine learning toolkit for Kubernetes that provides a comprehensive set of tools for building and deploying AI applications. Installing Kubeflow enables you to leverage its modular and scalable architecture to support various stages of the AI lifecycle.

steps.title: Add the Kubeflow Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Kubeflow Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kubeflow https://charts.kubeflow.org
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kubeflow using Helm
steps.description:
Next, install Kubeflow with the specified version in a new namespace called 'kubeflow'.
```bash
helm install kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --version v1.10.0 --namespace kubeflow --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all the pods are running in the 'kubeflow' namespace to ensure a successful installation.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeflow
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the Kubeflow components. You can edit the Helm release to set resource limits as needed.
```bash
helm upgrade kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --namespace kubeflow --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=1Gi
```

steps.title: Access the Kubeflow dashboard
steps.description:
Finally, set up port forwarding to access the Kubeflow dashboard locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/istio-ingressgateway -n kubeflow 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kubeflow will show all pods in the 'kubeflow' namespace as running. You can access the Kubeflow dashboard by navigating to http://localhost:8080 in your web browser.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubeflow https://charts.kubeflow.org
helm repo update
helm install kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --version v1.10.0 --namespace kubeflow --create-namespace
helm upgrade kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --namespace kubeflow --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=1Gi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Kubeflow installation, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command. This will remove all the resources associated with the release, but not the CRDs or persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall kubeflow --namespace kubeflow
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, you need to remove the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were installed with Kubeflow. This command will delete all CRDs related to Kubeflow. Additionally, if you have any persistent volumes (PVCs) that you want to clean up, you can delete them as well.
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kubeflow.org | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kubeflow
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'kubeflow' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. After deletion, verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubeflow
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Kubeflow installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kubeflow -o yaml > kubeflow-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, and then run the upgrade command to update your Kubeflow installation to the desired version. Replace 'v1.10.1' with the version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --version v1.10.1 --namespace kubeflow
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that all the pods are running in the 'kubeflow' namespace to ensure a successful upgrade.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that some pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, you can check the logs of the affected pod to diagnose the issue. Replace 'pod-name' with the actual name of the pod.
```bash
kubectl logs pod-name --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Kubeflow dashboard, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. You can check the service status with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource usage of the pods and nodes. You can use the following commands to diagnose:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace kubeflow
kubectl top nodes
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release failed to install
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm release fails to install, check the events in the namespace for any errors. This can provide insight into what went wrong during the installation process.
```bash
kubectl get events --namespace kubeflow
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed.
